Smart Interactive Virtual Guide

An AI-Powered Museum Guide Robot Built Under $30

Explore Project →
Get To Know Us

About The Project Team

We are a team of six final year Electronics & Telecommunication Engineering students from Sipna College of Engineering and Technology, Amravati, dedicated to creating innovative and affordable technology solutions.

Our project focuses on developing an intelligent museum guide robot that leverages cutting-edge embedded systems and cloud-based artificial intelligence to enhance visitor experiences in museums while keeping costs remarkably low.

Under the guidance of Dr. A.V.Malviya, we've successfully built a fully functional prototype that demonstrates the potential of accessible robotics and AI integration.

Project Overview

Abstract

Museums are increasingly adopting intelligent and cost-effective technologies to enhance visitor engagement and improve overall experience. This paper presents an AI-powered museum guide robot designed to provide real-time exhibit identification and interactive assistance to visitors.

The system leverages artificial intelligence techniques such as computer vision and speech recognition to understand exhibits and respond to user queries intelligently. By integrating visual perception with audio-based interaction, the robot delivers informative and engaging guidance in an autonomous manner.

  • 🎯 Cost-effective solution under $30 USD total BOM cost
  • 🤖 Dual ESP32 microcontroller architecture for efficient task distribution
  • 👁️ Cloud-based AI vision via Groq API (LLaMA-4)
  • 🎤 Voice recognition through ElevenLabs STT API
  • 📱 Web-based control interface accessible from any device
  • ⚡ Real-time response with sub-10-second latency
How It Functions

Working Principle

The guide robot operates on a highly coordinated sequence of edge-sensing, chunked data transmission, cloud AI analysis, and multimodal outputs. Below is the detailed step-by-step process of the system's operational pipeline.

01

Visual Capture & Pause

The camera subsystem handles real-time scene acquisition and buffer management.

  • Resolution: Captures JPEG frames at QVGA (320×240) to conserve heap.
  • Buffer Lock: Pauses the live MJPEG stream (port 81) to free frame buffers.
  • Direct Memory Access: Transfers frame data into internal DMA-capable SRAM.
02

Base64 Serialization

Converts binary image frames into text payloads suitable for web endpoints.

  • Base64 Coding: Encodes binary JPEGs into standard Base64 format.
  • Chunked Streaming: Streams payload to Groq endpoints in 2,048-byte chunks.
  • Heap Overflow Prevention: Eliminates the need to hold the entire payload in memory.
03

Cloud Vision Analysis

Leverages powerful cloud large language models for scene interpretation.

  • Groq Vision API: Transmits data via secure HTTPS to the LLaMA-4 Vision model.
  • Semantic Scene Interpretation: Processes context, colors, shapes, and spatial relationships.
  • Textual Response: Receives a complete natural language description of the scene.
04

Keyword Extraction & UART Trigger

Decides whether the camera view corresponds to a known museum exhibit.

  • String Matching: Scans the text response for target exhibit tags.
  • UART Command: Transmits an trigger command (e.g. `PLAY_GANESHA`) to the audio board.
  • Baud Rate: Runs over a hardware serial connection at 115,200 baud.
05

Voice Capture & VAD

Detects and captures spoken questions from visitors using edge audio metrics.

  • INMP441 Microphone: Collects audio via I2S protocol at 16 kHz sampling rate.
  • RMS Energy Calculation: Analyzes 512-sample frames for noise levels.
  • VAD State Machine: Confirms voice when 8 consecutive frames exceed 45 dB SPL.
06

Speech-to-Text Transcription

Transforms the visitor's voice query into structured text for processing.

  • ElevenLabs Scribe: Transmits 5-second raw audio files over HTTPS for transcription.
  • Alias Evaluation: Matches transcript against aliases (e.g. "mona lisa", "lisa").
  • Fallback Routine: Triggers fallback audio when queries match no keywords.
07

Synchronized Output

Delivers a synchronized, multimodal sensory guide experience to the visitor.

  • Audio Playback: Decodes pre-recorded MP3 from LittleFS via MAX98357A DAC.
  • OLED Scrolling: Renders description text dynamically on the SH1106 screen.
  • Web Sync: Pushes status details and summaries to the operator's browser UI.
08

Watchdog Protected Locomotion

Enables remote driving capabilities while prioritizing physical hardware safety.

  • Virtual Controls: Directs motors through the ESP32-CAM's web server interface.
  • Heartbeat Signals: Expects movement packets at a regular interval (300ms).
  • 500ms Watchdog: Instantly halts all motors if WiFi packets drop for over 500ms.

System Flowcharts

1. Image Analysis Pipeline

Operational workflow from visual scene trigger to output activation.

2. Voice Interaction Pipeline

Technical Design

System Architecture

Dual-Microcontroller Design Philosophy

The central pillar of our design is the deliberate separation of concerns across two independent ESP32 microcontrollers. A single ESP32 lacks the necessary resources (SRAM, PSRAM, and CPU cycles) to concurrently process real-time video streams, handle TCP/IP sockets for raw MJPEG data, run a local web server, and stream/decode digital audio over I2S.

By distributing tasks, the ESP32-CAM dedicates its dual cores to image capture, frame buffer serialization, and motor drive signals, while the ESP32 DevKit V1 processes high-frequency voice streams over I2S, manages local flash storage, and controls the graphical OLED interface.

📷 ESP32-CAM (Vision & Locomotion)

Handles all visual streaming, cloud visual uploads, and mobility drives.

Key Responsibilities:

  • MJPEG Video Server: Hosts live feed on Port 81 using direct DMA buffer reading.
  • Cloud Visual Uplink: Packages images in Base64 chunks and posts to Groq API.
  • Locomotion Control: Drives L298N motor driver using PWM signals.
  • HTTP Web Server: Serves the virtual controller dashboard on Port 80.

Pin Configurations:

  • L298N IN1 / IN2: GPIO 12 / GPIO 13
  • L298N IN3 / IN4: GPIO 15 / GPIO 14
  • UART communication: TX (GPIO 12) / RX (GPIO 13) (SoftSerial)

🎤 ESP32 DevKit V1 (Voice & Output)

Manages digital audio capturing, speech upload, locally saved MP3s, and the OLED status display.

Key Responsibilities:

  • Audio Input Sampling: Uses direct I2S DMA transfer to capture voice from INMP441 MEMS.
  • Sound Decoding: Decodes pre-recorded MP3 narratives from LittleFS flash file system.
  • I2S DAC Output: Plays digital audio through the MAX98357A amplifier.
  • GUI Renderer: Controls SH1106 OLED to show scrolling descriptions.

Pin Configurations:

  • INMP441 Microphone: SCK (GPIO 26) / WS (GPIO 25) / SD (GPIO 33)
  • MAX98357A DAC Amp: LRC (GPIO 27) / BCLK (GPIO 14) / DIN (GPIO 12)
  • SH1106 OLED Display: SCL (GPIO 22) / SDA (GPIO 21) (Hardware I2C)
  • UART Communication: TX (GPIO 17) / RX (GPIO 16) (Hardware Serial2)

🔗 Inter-Board Communication Protocol

To allow the camera module to trigger narration sequences on the audio module, the two microcontrollers are linked via a three-wire physical UART connection (Transmit, Receive, and Common Ground). The line operates at 115,200 baud with 8 data bits, no parity, and 1 stop bit (8N1 configuration).

Commands are sent as simple, carriage-return terminated string frames (e.g. "PLAY_GANESHA\n"). The audio board runs a continuous non-blocking buffer checks on its serial hardware register to immediately catch and execute these incoming UART commands.

Key System Components

ESP32-CAM Vision Module

Equipped with OV2640 camera, visual MJPEG server, locomotion PWM drive, and base64 cloud Vision API upload.

ESP32 DevKit V1

Master processor managing I2S digital MEMS sampling, audio synthesis playback, and SH1106 status screen.

L298N H-Bridge Driver

Dual H-Bridge power driver that controls speed and direction of DC drive wheels via PWM command lines.

INMP441 I2S MEMS Mic

High-sensitivity omnidirectional microphone delivering low-noise 24-bit audio directly over I2S lines.

Complete Schematic Circuit Diagram

This wiring blueprint illustrates the direct electrical link between the dual ESP32 cores, peripheral microchips (I2S mic/DAC, OLED screen), and the locomotion circuitry.

Critical Analysis

Pros & Cons

✓ Advantages

  • Extremely Cost-Effective: Total BOM under $30 USD vs $10K-$30K commercial alternatives
  • Practical AI Integration: Leverages cloud services without requiring local GPU hardware
  • High Accuracy: ~90% voice recognition and ~85% visual accuracy achieved
  • Modular Architecture: Dual-MCU design enables independent development and testing
  • User-Friendly Interface: Mobile-responsive web UI requires no app installation
  • Safety Features: Watchdog timer prevents runaway motor operation
  • Energy Efficient: 140mA idle consumption enables hours of battery life
  • Educational Value: Demonstrates integration of multiple technologies effectively
  • Scalable Design: Easy to add new exhibits or features via firmware updates
  • Open Source Components: Uses readily available off-the-shelf parts

✗ Limitations

  • Internet Dependency: Requires WiFi connectivity for cloud AI processing
  • Latency Issues: 5-10 second response time may feel slow for some users
  • No Obstacle Avoidance: Cannot navigate autonomously around obstacles
  • Limited Vocabulary: Voice recognition restricted to predefined keywords
  • Pre-recorded Audio: Cannot answer arbitrary questions dynamically
  • Single Language Support: Currently only supports English voice commands
  • Battery Life Constraints: 3.5 hours may be insufficient for full-day operation
  • Lighting Dependence: Camera performance varies with lighting conditions
  • No Offline Mode: Cannot function without internet connection
  • Manual Positioning: Requires operator to aim robot at exhibits
What's Next

Future Scopes

🤖

Autonomous Navigation

Integrate ultrasonic or LIDAR sensors for obstacle avoidance and self-guided tours, reducing operator workload significantly.

💬

Dynamic TTS Responses

Replace pre-recorded MP3 clips with real-time ElevenLabs Text-to-Speech to answer arbitrary visitor questions naturally.

⚡

Edge AI Deployment

Deploy lightweight YOLOv8-nano model via TensorFlow Lite Micro directly on ESP32 to reduce cloud dependency and improve privacy.

☁️

Cloud Content Management

Connect to cloud database allowing museum curators to remotely update exhibit information without firmware changes.

🌐

Multi-Language Support

Add support for regional languages like Hindi, Marathi, etc., making the system more inclusive and accessible to diverse visitors.

🧠

Advanced NLP Capabilities

Implement follow-up question handling and contextual conversation memory for more natural, engaging multi-turn interactions.
